topic: A student receives a much lower grade than expected
raw_output:
[story 1]

The red 'D-' swam at the top of my transcript, a stark, unforgiving stain against the crisp white paper. I stared at it, the ink blurring slightly at the edges. My hands, usually steady enough to ace any exam, felt clumsy, trembling slightly as I traced the number with a fingertip. The fluorescent lights of the library hummed with an irritating intensity, each buzz amplifying the hollow echo in my chest. I reread the feedback—a cascade of vague critiques that offered no real direction, just a cold assessment of failure. I pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal on linoleum sounding deafeningly loud. I needed air, but the thought of moving felt like trying to walk through water. My mouth went dry, and I swallowed hard, tasting only dust.

[story 2]

"A C-minus? For the entire semester?" The words felt alien, detached, as if they belonged to someone else entirely. I kept fiddling with the worn strap of my backpack, the familiar texture offering the only anchor in a suddenly shifting reality. Every movement seemed exaggerated, slow, as if my limbs were operating on a delayed signal. I walked to the window, the city outside a relentless, vibrant blur of motion. I watched a bus rush past, and for a moment, I couldn't process the movement; it was just a streak of color. A strange pressure built behind my eyes, a sensation like trying to hold too much static electricity. I leaned my forehead against the cool glass, trying to anchor myself to the solid frame of the building, but the world seemed to tilt just slightly off its axis.

[story 3]

I sat at my desk, the textbook open to the section where the grade resided, but the words refused to coalesce into meaning. It was just noise—a meaningless collection of symbols and low numbers. I picked up my pen, but the tip hovered over the page, refusing to make contact. My breathing became shallow, quick puffs that didn't seem to reach my lungs fully. I looked around the room, at the neatly organized notes, the familiar posters on the wall. Everything looked too sharp, too defined, and yet utterly unreal. A faint sheen of sweat prickled my palms. "This isn't right," I whispered, the sound thin and reedy in the quiet room. I squeezed my eyes shut, trying to force the sequence of events back into a logical order, but the gears in my mind were grinding against each other, refusing to mesh.
